exemplar.title:
To what extent can forest restoration efforts be leveraged to
improve soil quality in Eucalyptus-impacted areas of the Entoto mountain region
of Ethiopia?

detailedExemplarData.title:
To what extent can forest restoration efforts be leveraged to
improve soil quality in Eucalyptus-impacted areas of the Entoto mountain region
of Ethiopia?

strengths: Research question and topic are articulated with exceptional clarity and precision, anchoring the investigation firmly in both ecological and socioeconomic dimensions.
Consistent focus on the guiding question throughout the essay, with all sectionsâbackground, analysis, conclusionâdirectly linked to the stated topic.
Terminology defining key concepts (e.g., soil quality, allelopathy, forest restoration) is deployed accurately, reinforcing clear communication of the investigationâs scope.
weaknesses: Methodology justification, while appropriate, omits some procedural details (sampling frequency, equipment calibration) that would strengthen reproducibility.
Intermediate calculations (e.g., CV arithmetic) are not fully shown, reducing methodological transparency.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two of the three strands in Focus and Method comfortably meet the topâband (5â6) descriptors, with the research question and topic communicated clearly and consistently. The methodology selection is appropriate but missing some justificatory depth. Overall, the quality of focus and method warrants the highest available score of 6, reflecting a sharply defined investigation with only minor procedural omissions.

strengths: Knowledge is coherently integrated across Biology and ESS, with effective application of concepts like NPP, allelopathy and soil organic matter.
Technical terminology is used accurately and consistently, with definitions provided for most specialised terms.
Source selection includes a solid range of peer-reviewed journals, governmental reports and NGO materials relevant to the Entoto context.
weaknesses: Dependence on secondary Restor platform data limits the originality and expert selection of sources.
Some stakeholder perspectives are drawn from popular press without critical triangulation.
Minor inconsistencies in citation (e.g., missing source details for the 93% monoculture figure) detract from full source relevance.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Two strands (knowledge demonstration and use of terminology) clearly meet the 5â6 band descriptors, showing coherent integration and accurate conceptual use. Source material selection is mostly appropriate but not exemplary, aligning with the 3â4 band. A balanced overall score of 5 reflects strong command of subject matter with room for deeper primary sourcing.

strengths: Research draws on a broad, current and relevant literature base (satellite data, case studies, stakeholder interviews) with clear attention to data limitations.
Analysis integrates quantitative and qualitative evidence to support comparative evaluation of restoration strategies.
Argument is logically structured around an interdisciplinary framework, weighing ecological benefits against socioeconomic trade-offs.
weaknesses: Some interpretations (e.g., NPP decline solely due to allelopathy) lack evaluation of alternative explanations.
Conclusions would benefit from explicit uncertainty analysis and measurable restoration targets or timelines.
Transitions between quantitative ecology and social analysis occasionally rely on asserted rather than systematically evidenced links.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation demonstrates a high level of critical thinkingâpurposeful selection of relevant research (band 7â9), coherent analysis with largely supported conclusions (band 4â6), and a structured interdisciplinary argument (band 4â6). While not fully at the incisive depth of the topmost band, the workâs rigorous integration and largely well-supported reasoning justify a strong score of 10 out of 12.

strengths: Clear academic structure with logical headings and coherent flow through introduction, methods, analysis and conclusion.
Visual elements (maps, charts, SWOT diagram) are generally well-chosen and placed near relevant discussion.
Page numbers, word count and table of contents are correctly included.
weaknesses: Referencing style is inconsistent (APA-style vs. numeric footnotes), with broken URLs and missing access dates for digital sources.
Figure numbering and captions contain errors (duplicate figure numbers, omitted labels, missing interpretive summaries).
Units are missing in tables (e.g., seedlings/mÂ²), and some visuals (low-resolution images) reduce readability.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation exhibits a coherent academic structure and appropriate visuals (3â4 band), but persistent referencing and formatting inconsistencies place it below the top band. A score of 3 out of 4 accurately reflects solid organization tempered by notable presentation flaws.

strandSelections.reasoning:
The essay follows a logical comparative structure: background â method descriptions â dual-lens evaluations â SWOT syntheses â integrated conclusion.  Arguments are generally coherent, and the student shows some critical insight by weighing ecological benefits against socioeconomic costs and acknowledging stakeholder conflicts.

Evaluation is presentâfor instance, limitations of Restor data and financial feasibility of reintroduction are candidly discussed.  Nonetheless, some sections (e.g., farmer perspectives, implementation timetable) are under-developed, and transitions between biological data and social analysis occasionally rely on assertion rather than systematic evidence.

The presence of a clear, interdisciplinary thread and balanced, if sometimes uneven, evaluation corresponds to the upper level of the 4-6 band.

In-text citations are present, and a reference list is included; however, citation style fluctuates between APA-like and numeric footnotes, and some URLs are repeated or broken across lines.  The generic title âExtended Essayâ contravenes IB formatting guidance that requires the specific research title on the cover.

Page numbers, word count and table of contents are supplied, yet inconsistencies such as figure numbering (two Figure 8s) and missing units in Table 1 show only partial adherence to formatting conventions.  Hence the work fits the 1-2 band, warranting a score of 2.

The essay is organised with a conventional academic structure: title page, abstract substitute, table of contents, introduction, methodology, body sections with sub-headings, conclusion and references.  Headings are logical and aid reader navigation; visual aids are embedded near relevant text.

Minor structural issues persist: the overall title remains generic (âExtended Essayâ), some headings (e.g., separate RQ repetition) could be streamlined, and the SWOT analyses are embedded as images rather than clearly formatted tables.  Nevertheless, the structure is broadly coherent and meets formal expectations, aligning with the 3-4 bandâs lower range.

The essay demonstrates a coherent grasp of ecological conceptsâAGB, SOM, allelopathy, NPPâand applies them effectively to Entoto.  Calculations such as coefficient of variation are correctly executed, and figures are interpreted to substantiate claims about species dominance and soil degradation.

Knowledge from Biology and ESS is woven into socioeconomic analysis (e.g., linking water table impacts to crop yield).  Case-study comparisons are used to transfer ecological lessons from Brazil and New Zealand to Ethiopia, evidencing thoughtful application rather than mere description.

Minor conceptual gaps (limited quantified soil data) and occasional oversimplification of complex farmer perspectives prevent full mastery, but the overall integration of knowledge is strong, meriting the 5â6 band.

The analysis integrates quantitative data (AGB, NPP, CV calculations) with qualitative stakeholder insights to compare two restoration strategies.  Conclusions are largely supported by evidence and demonstrate awareness of trade-offs between ecological gains and social impacts.

However, some interpretations remain mainly qualitative (e.g., linking NPP decline solely to allelopathy without ruling out other factors) and lack precise, measurable restoration targets or timelines.  The conclusion synthesises but does not fully evaluate uncertainties or alternative explanations.

Coherence and support place the work firmly in the 4-6 band; the depth and incisiveness needed for the 7-9 band are not consistently evident, so a top-of-band score of 6 is appropriate.

The research questionââTo what extent can forest restoration efforts be leveraged to improve soil quality in Eucalyptus-impacted areas of the Entoto mountain region of Ethiopia?ââis concise, sharply focused and integrative, drawing explicit attention to both ecological and socioeconomic dimensions.  It provides a clear framework for the ensuing comparative analysis of two restoration approaches.

All subsequent sections (background, methodology, analysis and conclusion) return to this single guiding question, demonstrating its centrality and focus.  No elements of the investigation fall outside its stated scope, and key terms such as âsoil quality,â âEucalyptus-impacted,â and âforest restoration effortsâ are defined or operationalised within the essay.

Because the question is neither too broad nor ambiguous and directly steers the investigation, the strand comfortably attains the highest performance band.

The investigation draws on an extensive body of literature, satellite-derived datasets, case studies and stakeholder interviews, most of which are current and germane to the research problem.  The student critically flags data limitations (e.g., Restor estimates, lab-based allelopathy studies) and attempts triangulation with published field studies.

While a handful of popular sources are used, they mainly supplement stakeholder perspectives rather than provide core scientific evidence.  Overall, the majority of research is purposefully selected to illuminate both ecological and socioeconomic facets, reflecting a highâthough not flawlessâlevel of rigour and relevance, consistent with the 7-9 bandâs lower range.

The student selects two complementary methodsâselective harvesting/intercropping and reintroduction of endangered speciesâand explains data gathering via the Restor platform, case-study comparison, and stakeholder literature review.  These choices are generally appropriate for answering the question and show interdisciplinary thinking.

Justification, however, is uneven.  While the limitations of Restor data and financial constraints of Tahi-style reintroductions are acknowledged, the rationale for combining biological and ESS lenses is stated only briefly and lacks depth.  Details on how quantitative soil indicators will be measured beyond secondary data are sparse, and farmer perspectives remain under-sampled despite being identified as key stakeholders.

Overall, the methodology is fit for purpose and partly justified but exhibits gaps in depth and balance, aligning with the 3-4 band.

The bibliography shows a solid range of peer-reviewed journals (e.g., Journal of Applied Ecology, Science of the Total Environment), governmental reports, university theses, and NGO articles.  Sources span biological, ecological and socioeconomic angles and are generally pertinent to Entoto.

Nevertheless, dependence on the Restor platform for key soil metrics is heavy, and primary data specific to the study area are largely absent.  Some stakeholder statements come from popular press without critical triangulation.  These weaknesses prevent the material from reaching an âexpertly chosenâ standard but still reflect mostly relevant, appropriate selectionâconsistent with the 3-4 band.

The essay incorporates numerous figures: satellite maps, systems diagrams, bar charts, line graphs, photos, and SWOT matrices.  Captions are provided and sources cited.  Most visuals are legible and directly support the analysis (e.g., Figure 4 AGB chart, Figure 5 NPP trend).

A few shortcomings remain: Table 1 omits units for âSeedlings/mÂ²,â and some photographic images (e.g., Figure 7) add limited analytical value.  Overall, the selection and labelling are adequate and clearly linked to discussion, fulfilling the criteria for the 3-4 band.

Technical languageâe.g., âallelopathy,â ânet primary productivity,â ânon-pioneer native species,â âcoefficient of variationââis used accurately and consistently.  Definitions are supplied for most specialised terms, aiding clarity for a non-expert reader.

Key biological and ESS concepts are deployed precisely when analysing data and framing arguments, indicating sound conceptual understanding.  Occasional missed opportunities to define lesser-known socioeconomic terms (e.g., nuances of âlivelihood securityâ) slightly limit accessibility but do not undermine overall command.

Given the breadth, correctness and consistency of terminology across disciplines, the work meets the expectations of the 5-6 band.

annotations.comment: The student references two case studies but does not justify why Brancalion et al. and the Tahi project were selected. A rationale connecting these studies to the Entoto context would strengthen critical evaluation.
The definition of selective harvesting is concise and accurate, demonstrating solid knowledge of forestry concepts and linking them effectively to restoration methodology.

While limitations of the Restor platform are noted, the student does not discuss potential biases from satellite data resolution or groundâtruthing gaps. A deeper critique of data sources would enhance the investigationâs critical rigor.
Footnote URLs are repeatedly broken across lines and styled inconsistently. The student should adopt a uniform citation format to improve readability and adhere to referencing conventions.
Figure 1âs caption correctly cites the Restor source but omits the access date. Including the date of retrieval is essential for digital resources to ensure reference precision.

The cover title remains the generic âExtended Essayâ rather than the specific investigation title. The student should replace this with the full research title to meet IB formatting requirements and clearly communicate the studyâs focus.

The introduction effectively frames the interdisciplinary topic by linking key biological and socioeconomic dimensions. This clearly communicates the investigationâs scope and aligns with the focus and method criterionâs top band.

The methodology overview lists broad approaches but omits details on sampling frequency, equipment calibration, and data validation protocols. The student should specify these methodological steps to fully justify method selection and strengthen reproducibility.
The abbreviation âSOMâ is used before its full term âsoil organic matterâ has been defined. Define acronyms at first use to ensure clarity and demonstrate accurate use of terminology.
Citation style fluctuates between numeric footnotes and authorâdate references. The student should standardize all inâtext citations and bibliography entries to a single consistent style.
The 93% monoculture figure is compelling but no primary data or source details are given. The student should reference the original data source and discuss its scope to demonstrate source relevance.
Figure 2âs caption describes map content but provides no interpretive insight. A brief summary of the key takeaway (e.g., dominant land uses) would enhance the figureâs analytical value.
The map of Entoto Park is detailed and wellâlabelled, providing clear geospatial context that supports subsequent ecological and socioeconomic analyses.
Figure 6âs pie chart uses small text and relies solely on color to distinguish segments. The student should label each segment directly and consider colorblindâsafe palettes for accessibility.
In Table 3, the species name âEucalyptus globulousâ appears misspelled. Correct taxonomic names to maintain scientific accuracy and credibility.

The coefficient of variation formula is described in prose rather than LaTeX. Present equations using LaTeX syntax (e.g., $$CV = \frac{\sigma}{\mu}$$) to align with IB expectations for mathematical clarity.
The CV calculation for Entoto yields an unusually high value; the student should re-check the arithmetic and explicitly show intermediate steps to ensure reproducibility.
The systems lens considers farmers and women but omits other stakeholders (e.g., park management, policymakers). A broader stakeholder map would deepen the socioeconomic analysis.

The account of Pedro et al.âs treatments omits discussion of experimental design elements (e.g., replication, randomization). Including these details would allow readers to assess the robustness of the cited study.

The analysis attributes NPP decline solely to allelopathy without considering other possible drivers such as soil compaction or nutrient leaching. The student should discuss alternate explanations to fully address the research question.
Table 1 omits units for âSeedlings/mÂ².â The student must include proper units and clarify any error margins to ensure table data are interpretable and conform to presentation standards.

Figure 5âs data table and graph round to inconsistent significant figures. The student should apply uniform precision (e.g., two significant digits) to maintain data clarity and presentation quality.

Figure 7 is visually appealing but does not add analytical value. The student should either integrate observational insights from the image into the analysis or replace it with a figure that provides quantitative support.

The SWOT analysis is embedded as a lowâresolution image. Converting it into a formatted table with text would improve readability, accessibility, and consistency with academic presentation standards.
Table 1 extrapolates seedling estimates but provides no confidence intervals or error metrics. Including these statistical measures would bolster the credibility of the regeneration data.
Figure 9âs caption lacks a clear label (e.g., âFigure 9: ...â). Consistent figure numbering and descriptive captions are essential for reader navigation and presentation coherence.

Health impacts on women are described in detail but lack citation of primary health data or studies. Referencing epidemiological or occupational health research would strengthen the socioeconomic argument.
Method 2âs SWOT is informative but appears as an image. Converting it into a formatted table would improve accessibility and allow clearer crossâanalysis with Method 1âs SWOT.
Figure 10 is introduced without text guidance. The student should describe the key findings in the main text (e.g., percentages and categories) to integrate visual data into the narrative.
The conclusion introduces political feasibility without prior analysis in the body. Incorporating political considerations earlier would create a more cohesive argument structure.

The claim about âextentâ remains qualitative. The student should link back to quantitative indicators (NPP trends or CV values) to provide a measurable assessment of how well the approach meets objectives.
The environmental conclusion could cite specific NPP improvement targets or soil quality benchmarks to show how the proposed combined method would achieve measurable ecological gains.

The references section omits entries cited in the text (e.g., Science of the Total Environment). Ensure every inâtext citation has a corresponding bibliography entry for completeness and presentation consistency.
